A study was completed to develop procedures for fabricating thin-walled cylinders of $\mathrm{Gd}_{2}\mathrm{O}_{3}$ that contain up to 30 wt $\%$ $\mathrm{Al}_{2}\mathrm{O}_{3}$ .
These cylinders are of interest as control rod elements for the MSRE, and their composition was designed to withstand moisture in the atmosphere at temperatures up to $1400^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
The $\mathrm{Al}_{2}\mathrm{O}_{3}$ was added to minimize hydrolysis and subsequent deterioration of the $\mathrm{Gd}_{2}\mathrm{O}_{3}$ .

In preliminary work, $^{9}$ severe distortion resulted from heating cold-pressed mechanical mixtures of $70\% \mathrm{Gd}_{2}\mathrm{O}_{3} - 30\% \mathrm{Al}_{2}\mathrm{O}_{3}$ to $1650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , indicating the formation of a low-temperature-melting compound, identified by x-ray diffraction analysis as the primary perovskite-type phase $\mathrm{GdAlO_3}$ .
By this technique it was possible to sinter at temperatures up to $1645^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and to obtain undistorted pieces with bulk densities of 5.1 to $5.2\mathrm{g/cm}^3$ .

Densities to $5.26\mathrm{g/cm}^3$ were attained, with minor dimensional changes, by thermal cycling the parts between 1635 and $1660^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ; however, this technique is not considered practical for making large quantities of the material.

A prereacted mixture of 80 wt $\%$ $\mathrm{Gd}_{2}\mathrm{O}_{3} - 20$ wt $\%$ $\mathrm{Al}_{2}\mathrm{O}_{3}$ was also investigated using similar techniques, except that the mixture was reacted initially at $1750^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ rather than at $1650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
No deformation or tendency to weld was observed in specimens sintered at temperatures as high as $1750^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for 1 hr.
An x-ray diffraction analysis of these materials revealed $\mathrm{GdAlO}_{3}$ perovskite and an, as yet, unidentified phase.
After the graphite cores were removed from capsules 45 and 12 and examined, they were stored in screw-cap plastic bottles for five months.
The gas samples were taken following periods of at least 1 hr each at 600, 800, and $1000^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

In the case of capsule 45, the xenon released from the graphite at the successive temperatures was 0.031, 0.025, and $0.055~\mathrm{cm}^3$ (STP), totaling 0.111 cm³ or 7.5% of the calculated $1.5\text{-cm}^3$ yield from fissioning.

A considerable amount of $\mathrm{SiF_4}$ ( $\sim 3~\mathrm{cm}^3$ ) was also in the gas; the source of the silicon, other than what might have come from the stainless steel vacuum tank and lines and from the glass manifold, is not known.

In the case of capsule 12, there was a small air leak during the heating under vacuum; 0.065 cm³ of xenon was recovered at $600^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ along with insignificant amounts at higher temperatures, which again amounted to 7.5% of the calculated $0.87\text{-cm}^3$ yield from fissioning in capsule 12.
This time the fluorine appeared as $\mathrm{CF_4}$ ( $\sim 0.4~\mathrm{cm}^3$ ), and little $\mathrm{SiF_4}$ was noted, in contrast with the behavior in the absence of an air leak.

The $\mathrm{XeO_3}$ molecule has a dipole moment and should adsorb on graphite much more readily than the symmetrical $\mathrm{XeF_4}$ ; however, the dipole moment of $\mathrm{XeO_3}$ is probably not large because of the compensating effect of the unshared electrons on the xenon atom at the apex of a triangular pyramid with three oxygens at the base at $\mathrm{Xe - O}$ distances of 1.76 A and 0-Xe-0 angles of $103^{\circ}$ .

Graphite from an unirradiated control capsule was subjected to the same procedure as the irradiated graphite, and the gas ( $\sim 2 \mathrm{~cm}^3$ total) was predominantly hydrogen, along with other common constituents from the degassing of graphite.

As recovered from the heating in stainless steel apparatus, the graphite from capsule 45 was found to be coated with a silvery film resembling in appearance some previously encountered surface deposits of chromium and/or iron carbides on graphite.

Because of the repeated cycling through strongly oxidizing and reducing cycles as a consequence of $\mathbf{F}_2$ generation during reactor shutdowns, the contents of capsules from assembly ORNL-MTR-47-4 had an uncertain environmental history.
Large pieces, 0.8 g or larger, in which the surface-to-mass ratio was similar to that of the original, were dissolved in a boiling mixture of sulfuric and nitric acids.
The analyses presented in Table 5.1 show increasing uranium contents with capsule power, which in turn correlates with the intensity of the off-equilibrium cycles associated with shutdowns.

Graphite from capsules 12, 45, and 3A was subjected to bakeout of gases at $1000^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , which may have caused changes in the constituents analyzed, and graphite from capsule 36 was not analyzed because it was involved in an attempt to recover fuel by melting.

Speculations regarding the mechanism by which uranium occurred in the graphite have dealt mainly with two possibilities: (1) Transport of $\mathrm{UF}_6$ seemed possible as a consequence of the $\mathbf{F}_2$ atmosphere, but graphite alone was not regarded an adequate reducing agent for fixation of $\mathrm{UF}_6$ in the cores.
(2) Strongly reduced salt was probably in brief contact with the graphite during startup periods before the released $\mathbf{F}_2$ could be re-equilibrated into the melt.

Conditions during the accumulation periods ranged from ambient temperature, during shutdowns, to temperatures of $815^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ at fission-power densities of 85 w/cm $^3$ in LiF-BeF $_2$ -ZrF $_4$ -UF $_4$ (~67-28-4-0.67 mole%).

Noticeably greater sensitivity of measurement was attained on the later reanalyses, where radioactivity was longer a problem; in general, the latest figures were adopted as most representative, although again there were large fluctuations from sample to sample in the amount that could have been present (below the limit of detection) in the 18 samples that gave negative results.

The amounts of $\mathrm{CF_4}$ in a sample were presented as ratios of $\mathrm{CF_4}$ to xenon in the same sample for the following reasons: (1) the primary generation of $\mathrm{CF_4}$ is probably strongly influenced by the energy, as is of course certainly true of xenon; (2) comparison of mass spectrometric peaks on the same sample eliminates many possible discrepancies that might stem from unrecognized differences in sampling; and (3) also a convenient reference factor is available for assessing the chemical effects on fuels of any given loss of fluoride as $\mathrm{CF_4}$ .

Both capsules were evacuated after the $87^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ interval that ended on day 95, and fluorine evolution with no added pressure was followed for 15 days at $33^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ to furnish a "baseline".

The observations under these conditions were extended over 15 days because the evolution rate continued to rise slowly, in line with previous observations that room-temperature rates were not immediately recovered after the salt had been heated.
On heating to $83^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , the 0.67 mole $\%$ UF $_4$ salt rapidly consumed the generated F $_2$ , the pressure falling from 0.39 psia to 0.07 psia.
In the same interval the generation rate in the 0.34 mole $\%$ UF $_4$ capsule slowly dropped to zero, confirming previous behavior.
On day 113, the temperature was raised to $88^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , the capsules were evacuated, and 1 psia of fresh F $_2$ was added to each.
The initial consumption rate in the 0.67 mole $\%$ UF $_4$ capsule with higher reduction more than doubled its value for 0.39 psia and then decreased.
Fluorine was again added to 1 psia, and the consumption rate nearly reproduced the previous gyration.
At 1 psia and $88^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , the 0.34 mole $\%$ UF $_4$ salt slowly consumed F $_2$ .
The capsules were sampled and evacuated, and 0.5 atm of $\mathbf{F}_2$ was added to each.
The more concentrated fuel consumed $\mathbf{F}_2$ at approximately twice the l-psia rate for three days, then less rapidly as the $\mathbf{F}_2$ pressure decreased to 0.25 atm.

Again the consumption rate in the concentrated fuel did not quite reach the previous maximum rate at 0.5 atm $\mathbb{F}_2$ , possibly because salt accessible to $\mathbb{F}_2$ had become partially saturated.
On day 122, the assembly temperature was reduced to $34^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , leaving slightly less than 1 atm of $\mathbf{F}_2$ in each capsule.

Curiously, there was now little if any change in rate in 12 days of continued evolution; otherwise, the addition of $\mathbf{F}^{-}$ to the fluoride-deficient salt apparently had not enhanced subsequent $\mathbf{F}_2$ liberation.
In fact, the slightly lower rates could perhaps have been considered indicative of slight back-reaction at 1 atm of $\mathbf{F}_2$ at $34^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
With the $\mathbf{F}_2$ pressure maintained near $1\mathrm{atm}$ , the capsule temperature was raised to $54^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
The 0.67 mole $\%$ $\mathbf{UF_4}$ capsule began to consume $\mathbf{F}_2$ slowly.
When the temperature was raised to $74^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , the 0.34 mole $\%$ $\mathbf{UF_4}$ salt showed another small $\mathbf{F}_2$ burst and then leveled at a low positive generation rate.

The 0.67 mole $\%$ $\mathbf{UF_4}$ salt consumed $\mathbf{F}_2$ at a moderate rate at $74^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , showing an erratic rate behavior; such irregular behavior is common in gas-solid reactions involving successive formation and rupture of partially protective films.

Finally, when the temperature was again raised to $88^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , the consumption rate for the more-reduced salt increased to a value only one-fourth its previous value at $88^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and 1 atm $\mathbf{F}_2$ and then fell off strongly.
At $88^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , the 0.34 mole $\%$ $\mathbf{UF_4}$ salt gave off another slow $\mathbf{F}_2$ burst that lasted more than two days before consumption dominated.
It appeared that the 0.34 mole $\%$ $\mathbf{UF_4}$ capsule was more prone to such bursts at 1 atm of $\mathbf{F}_2$ overpressure than at 1 psia overpressure.
The capsules were again sampled, evacuated, and cooled to $38^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
(The measured pressure drops were only 0.03 and 0.05 psia out of a total pressure of 14.7 psia.) For the remaining three days, approximately normal generation rates were observed.

MSRE salt, chemically reduced to the extent corresponding to 3.5 to $4\%$ fluorine loss, reacted with fluorine at a rate which was very low at room temperature, easily measurable at $54^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , and
rapidly increasing as the temperature exceeded $88^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
With salt reduced only to $2\%$ fluorine loss, the recombination was measurable only above $88^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
Continued reaction of fluorine with the strongly reduced salt resulted in a very marked decrease of consumption rate before even $0.5\%$ of its $4\%$ fluorine deficit had been restored.

In extrapolating the above conclusions, temperatures well above $100^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ would probably be required in order to restore radiolyzed MSRE salt to its original oxidation state by fluorination.
As reaction continued, the reaction order fell to 0.5 and less.
The salt had cooled 150 days at the time of testing, and the high-power capsule should have been generating beta activity at the rate of 0.1 w.

Fluorine generation is expected to be negligibly slow from solid MSRE fuel salt which had been fissioning at $65\mathrm{~W/cm}^3$ , provided its temperature is maintained above, e.g., $200^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

The net fluorine liberation rate depends on the balance of at least three effects: (1) the primary radiolytic generation rate, which is probably temperature independent; (2) chemical recombination of fluorine inside the crystals with the reduced radiolytic species, e.g., $\mathrm{Li}^{\mathrm{O}}$ , $\mathrm{Be}^{\mathrm{O}}$ , $\mathrm{U}^{3+}$ , and $\mathrm{U}^{\mathrm{O}}$ ; and (3) a temperature-dependent diffusion step for escape of internal $\mathbf{F}^{\mathrm{O}}$ or $\mathbf{F}_2$ from the crystal.

One phase of the experiments exploring this problem consisted in exposing such a fuel salt, otherwise unirradiated but in contact with graphite, to gamma irradiation in the 10,000-curie Co $^{60}$ facility of the ORNL Chemical Technology Division.

A solution of $1\mathrm{g}$ of MSRE fuel in $100\mathrm{ml}$ of $0.5\mathrm{M}\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{SO}_4$ was used as the test solution.
Into a 50-ml flask was pipetted $4.5\mathrm{ml}$ of the test solution containing $39\mu \mathrm{g}$ of nickel.

The reagent solutions were added, with slurrying between each addition, as follows: $30\mathrm{w / v}\%$ ammonium citrate, $5\mathrm{ml}$ ; saturated potassium persulfate, $10\mathrm{ml}$ ; after $10\mathrm{min}$ , concentrated ammonium hydroxide, $10\mathrm{ml}$ ; $1\mathrm{w / v}\%$ dimethylglyoxime in $95\%$ alcohol, $2\mathrm{ml}$ ; and distilled water (amount required to make final volume to $50\mathrm{ml}$ ).

The solution was allowed to stand for $30\mathrm{min}$ , and the absorbancy was measured against a blank prepared in the same manner, including the test solution but without the dimethylglyoxime reagent.
The absorbancy was obtained with the remotely operated filter photometer, fitted with a $450 - \mu \mathrm{m}$ filter.
